card.title: Compound Interest: The 8th Wonder
card.teaser: Einstein supposedly called it the most powerful force in the universe. Here's why a boring formula quietly mints millionaires.
card.coverImagePrompt: A golden coin multiplying into a towering stack that curves upward like an exponential graph, warm sunset light, cinematic and slightly surreal

lessons.title: A penny that doubles
lessons.body: Would you rather have a million dollars right now, or a single penny that doubles every day for 30 days? Most people pick the million. They're wrong. The penny becomes over $5 million. That's compound interest — and once it gets rolling, it's almost embarrassing how fast it grows.

visual.imagePrompt: A single penny on day 1 beside a mountain of coins on day 30, split-screen, dramatic lighting

lessons.title: Interest earning interest
lessons.body: Simple interest pays you a fixed amount each year based on your original deposit. Compound interest pays you interest on your interest — last year's gains join this year's principal, and the whole snowball grows. The longer it rolls, the bigger the snowball, the more snow it picks up.

keyTerms.definition: The original amount of money deposited or invested
Earning returns on both the principal and previously earned interest

visual.imagePrompt: A small snowball rolling downhill, growing into a boulder, in a wintery pastel scene

lessons.title: The Rule of 72
lessons.body: Here's a trick bankers have used for centuries. Divide 72 by your annual rate of return to find how many years it takes your money to double. At 6%, that's 12 years. At 8%, nine years. At 10%, just over seven. It's not exact, but it's close enough that you can run the math in your head at a dinner table.

visual.imagePrompt: A notebook page showing '72 / 8% = 9 years', with handwritten examples and a small abacus nearby

lessons.title: Starting early beats investing more
lessons.body: Imagine two people. Anna invests $200/month from age 25 to 35, then stops. Ben invests $200/month from 35 to 65 — three times as long. At a 7% return, who ends up richer at 65? Anna. Her early decade compounded for 40 years. Ben's money never got the same head start. Time matters more than the amount you contribute.
lessons.funFact: Warren Buffett made roughly 99% of his wealth after his 50th birthday — not because he got better at investing, but because compounding had decades to work.

visual.imagePrompt: Two silhouettes with growing money trees beside them, Anna's tree far taller despite her shorter watering time

lessons.title: Time is the quiet ingredient
lessons.body: You don't need a big paycheck or a stock-picking talent. You need time, patience, and the discipline to leave it alone. The ugliest truth about getting rich is that it's boring — compound interest rewards the willingness to wait longer than most people are willing to.

quiz.question: What's the key difference between simple and compound interest?
options: Simple interest is taxed, compound isn't
Compound interest pays interest on previously earned interest
Simple interest is only used by banks
Compound interest requires a larger deposit
quiz.explanation: With compound interest, earned interest is added to the principal so future interest is calculated on the growing balance.

quiz.question: Using the Rule of 72, roughly how long does it take for money to double at an 8% annual return?
Why does starting to invest earlier often beat investing more later?
options: Older investments aren't allowed to compound
Early contributions have more years to compound
quiz.explanation: The longer money has to compound, the more exponentially it grows — time is the biggest multiplier.

sources.title: A Random Walk Down Wall Street
